children.name: Apteryx
children.description: Flightless, nocturnal birds endemic to New Zealand, characterised by vestigial wings, hairlike feathers and a long, curved bill with nostrils at the tip. They are the smallest of the ratites and lay the largest eggs relative to body size of any bird.

children.name: Apteryx mantelli
children.description: The most numerous kiwi species, found across the North Island of New Zealand. It is a reddish-brown bird with a long, slender bill that probes the forest floor for earthworms and invertebrates. Nocturnal and monogamous, pairs maintain territories in native forests and regenerating scrublands.

children.name: Apteryx owenii
children.description: The smallest kiwi species at just 1.2 kilograms, easily identified by its pale greyish-brown plumage spotted with white. Once widespread across New Zealand, it now survives only on offshore islands and in sanctuaries where introduced predators are absent. It feeds on small invertebrates and fallen fruit.

children.name: Apteryx haastii
children.description: The largest kiwi, standing about 45 centimetres tall and weighing up to 3.3 kilograms. Its plumage is patterned with grey-brown mottling and distinct white spots. Confined to the mountainous forests of the South Island's western regions, it uses its strong legs to dig for earthworms deep in the soil.

children.name: Apteryx rowi
children.description: A recently recognised species found only in the Okarito forest on the South Island's West Coast. It has greyish-brown plumage and is slightly larger than the Brown Kiwi. With fewer than 500 individuals remaining, it is critically endangered and the focus of intensive predator-control efforts.

children.name: Apteryx australis
children.description: Also known as the Tokoeka, this species inhabits the South Island and Stewart Island of New Zealand. It is similar in appearance to the North Island Brown Kiwi but has darker, more streaked plumage. Subspecies vary in size and habitat preference, from Fiordland's rainforests to Stewart Island's coastal dunes.
